summary: The case concerned the European Commission’s refusal to grant public access to an application for a mineral extraction and processing project to be recognised as a ‘Strategic Project’ under the Critical Raw Materials Act and the Commission’s related assessment. The Commission considered that disclosure would undermine the commercial interests of the company concerned, including because the project had not been designated as a Strategic Project. Amongst other elements, the complainant contended that there is an overriding public interest in disclosure, arguing that the documents at issue are likely to contain important environmental information. Based on the inspection of the documents at issue by her inquiry team, the Ombudsman found that it had been reasonable for the Commission to consider that disclosure would undermine the commercial interests of the company concerned. In addition, while the documents contain some information about the anticipated environmental and social impacts of the project, this was not sufficient to establish an overriding public interest in disclosure. The Ombudsman therefore closed the inquiry finding no maladministration in the Commission’s refusal of access.

title: Decision on how the European Commission followed-up on a judgment of the Court of Justice of the EU that Spain infringed EU law (case 2183/2024/(OAM)PGP)
text:
Background to the complaint 1. On 14 June 2017, the European Commission opened a formal infringement procedure against Spain by issuing a letter of formal notice [1] on the grounds that its national rules on State liability for a breach of EU law due to the Spanish legislature were contrary to the principles of equivalence and/or effectiveness.

2. On 25 January 2018, the Commission sent a reasoned opinion to Spain. [2] 3. On 27 November 2019, the Commission decided to refer Spain to the Court of Justice of the European Union (CJEU) regarding Spanish rules on compensation for damages caused by the State when adopting legislation contrary to Union law. [3] 4. On 28 June 2022, the CJEU adopted a judgment in case C-278/20 (‘the judgment’) in which it held that certain provisions, contained in two Spanish laws [4] and regulating the compensation for the loss or harm caused to individuals by the Spanish legislature as a result of an infringement of EU law, were themselves in breach of the principle of effectiveness.

5. In November 2024, the complainant turned to the Ombudsman [5] complaining about the time taken by the Commission to ensure Spain’s compliance with the judgment and its delay in referring the matter to the CJEU for a second time under Article 260(2) TFEU [6] . The inquiry 6. The Ombudsman opened an inquiry into the time taken by the Commission to ensure Spain’s compliance with the judgment.

7. In the course of the inquiry, the Ombudsman received the reply of the Commission to the complaint and, subsequently, the comments of the complainant in response to the Commission’s reply. The Ombudsman inquiry team also inspected electronically some documents contained in the Commission’s file on this case. Subsequently, the Ombudsman inquiry team met with representatives of the Commission and inspected additional documents during the meeting. The Ombudsman invited the complainant to provide comments on the inspection report concerning the documents inspected electronically and on the meeting report and the complainant did so. Arguments presented to the Ombudsman By the Commission 8. The following timeline on the Commission’s handling of the matter was presented during the inquiry: On 27 October 2022, the Commission sent an administrative letter to Spain. Between 28 November 2022 and 2 May 2023, Spain sent three letters to the Commission. On 23 July 2023, general elections took place in Spain. On 15 November 2023, a new Prime Minister was inaugurated in Spain. Between 29 July 2024 and 2 October 2024, the Commission and Spain held two meetings. On 7 January 2025, Spain sent draft legislative amendments to the Commission aimed at complying with the judgment. On 10 February 2025, the Commission sent an e-mail to Spain. On 14 March 2025, Spain sent new draft legislative amendments to the Commission. On 18 March 2025, Spain and the Commission held a meeting. On 26 March 2025 and then again on 7 April 2025, Spain sent new draft legislative amendments to the Commission On 23 April 2025, Spain and the Commission held a meeting. On 24 April 2025, Spain sent new draft legislative amendments to the Commission. On 30 April 2025, the Spanish Council of Ministers sent its preliminary draft law to the Council of State. On 7 October 2025, the Spanish Council of Ministers adopted the draft law and sent it to the Spanish Congress of Deputies. On 8 October 2025, the Commission requested Spain to provide explanatory information. On 30 October 2025, Spain sent some information to the Commission. On 6 November 2025, Spain sent further information to the Commission. On 10 November 2025, the Commission sent an e-mail to Spain. On 11 December 2025, the Commission sent, pursuant to Article 260(2) TFEU, a letter of formal notice to Spain. On 17 December 2025, the Commission sent a letter to the complainant.

9. The Commission explained that, since the delivery of the judgment, it had been in contact with the Spanish authorities. Several exchanges took place during the period 27 October 2022 to 2 May 2023. Internal discussions took place within the Commission between July 2023 and July 2024 and some requests were sent to Spain during this period. Between July 2024 and March 2025, three meetings were held between representatives of the Commission and representatives of Spain regarding the measures taken by Spain to comply with the judgment. Spain sent draft legislative amendments to the Commission on four occasions during the period January to April 2025.

10. The Commission considered the preliminary draft law that Spain shared with it on 24 April 2025 to implement the judgment. However, on 7 October 2025, the draft law, adopted by the Spanish Council of Ministers and sent to the Spanish Congress of Deputies, included some changes compared to the preliminary draft law of April 2025. Considering that the new changes contributed to the delay in ensuring Spain’s compliance with the judgment, the Commission sent Spain a letter of formal notice in December 2025.

11. Regarding the time taken, the Commission explained that, according to its internal guidelines, its services must investigate cases concerning Member States’ failure to comply with CJEU judgments (Article 260 TFEU cases) with a view to arriving at a decision to refer the matter to the CJEU for a second time or to close the case within 8 to 18 months. However, the time taken to come to a conclusion depends on each specific case. If there is a willingness from the Member State concerned to advance in the legislative changes needed to comply with the CJEU judgment, it is more efficient to continue the dialogue with the Member State rather than to immediately send a letter of formal notice under Article 260(2) TFEU. In this case, the delay in ensuring Spain’s compliance with the judgment was also affected by the re-assignment of the infringement procedure between different Commission departments and the general elections that took place in Spain in July 2023, which halted the measures taken by Spain to comply with the judgment until there was a new government. By the complainant 12. The complainant stated that Spanish citizens are in a situation of legal uncertainty when it comes to making claims for compensation due to loss or harm caused to individuals by the Spanish legislature as a result of an infringement of EU law. More than three years since the delivery of the judgment, Spain has not yet brought its legislation in line with EU law to comply with the judgment. According to EU case-law [7] , a Member State cannot plead provisions, practices or situations prevailing in its domestic legal order to justify failure to observe obligations arising under EU law.

13. According to EU case-law, the process of compliance by the Member State concerned with a CJEU judgment must be initiated at once and must be completed as soon as possible. [8] In the complainant’s opinion, the Commission does not have discretion in ensuring compliance by the Member State concerned with the CJEU judgment.

14. The Commission has failed to comply with its internal guidelines that foresee that cases where Member States have failed to comply with a CJEU judgment must be pursued as a matter of priority and that the Commission must investigate those cases with a view to arriving at a decision to refer the matter to the CJEU for a second time or to close the case within 8 to 18 months. The Commission must comply with its internal guidelines to ensure administrative coherence, transparency and to avoid the perception of arbitrariness. The Commission’s internal guidelines create a legitimate expectation that they will be applied and they impose limits on the Commission’s discretion.

15. In the case at stake, the Spanish government is using tactics to delay Spain’s compliance with the judgment. The delay is also due to the Commission’s failure to comply with its responsibilities.

16. According to the complainant, all the documentation that the Spanish Council of Ministers sent to the Spanish Congress of Deputies in October 2025 dates back to 2023, which shows that the file has been dormant for a couple of years. Moreover, the complainant further argued that the Spanish Congress of Deputies has been postponing multiple times the deadline for submitting amendments to the draft law of October 2025. The Ombudsman's assessment 17. The role of the Ombudsman in this case is to determine whether the time taken by the Commission to ensure Spain’s compliance with the judgment is justified and compatible with the principles of good administration.

18. The Commission’s handling of this matter relates to Spain’s compliance with a CJEU judgment. Even though the EU Treaties do not specify the period within which a CJEU judgment must be complied with, it follows from EU case-law that the importance of immediate and uniform application of EU law requires that the process of compliance by the Member State concerned must be initiated at once and must be completed as soon as possible. [9] The CJEU delivered its judgment on 28 June 2022 and Spain has not yet brought its legislation in line with EU law to comply with the judgment.

19. According to Article 260(2) TFEU, when the Member State concerned has not taken the necessary measures to comply with the CJEU judgment, the Commission may bring the case before the CJEU after giving that Member State the opportunity to submit its observations. While the Commission therefore has discretion in deciding whether and when to make use of the power conferred to it by Article 260(2) TFEU, the Ombudsman considers that it should, as a matter of good administration, be diligent and active in monitoring the compliance of the Member State concerned with the judgment. The Commission should do so in accordance with its internal guidelines, within a reasonable time and without undue delays.

20. The Ombudsman’s view on this matter is supported by the Commission’s internal guidelines of 18 July 2017 “Monitoring the application of EU law”, which set out that the Commission will normally pursue, as a matter of priority, cases where Member States have failed to comply with a CJEU judgment. The same internal guidelines foresee that the Commission services must investigate those cases with a view to arriving at a decision to refer the matter to the CJEU for a second time or to close the case within 8 to 18 months.

21. The Commission first contacted Spain to enquire about the implementation of the judgment on 27 October 2022, that is four months after the judgment. Between November 2022 and May 2023, Spain sent replies to the Commission. Given that implementing the judgment appeared to require legislative amendments, which takes time, these initial steps appear reasonable.

22. Between May 2023 and July 2024, the documents inspected in the Commission’s file do not indicate any action by the Commission. Although the Commission argues that internal discussions took place within the Commission during this period and that some requests were sent to Spain, no documentation to substantiate this exists in the Commission’s file. However, based on the information gathered during the inquiry, it appears that the delay in progressing on the matter during this period can be partly attributed to the situation in Spain. The general elections, which took place in Spain in July 2023, halted the measures taken by Spain to comply with the judgment until there was a new government in November 2023.

23. Between July 2024 and April 2025, four meetings were held between representatives of Spain and representatives of the Commission. In addition, between January 2025 and April 2025, Spain sent draft legislative amendments to the Commission on four occasions. This shows that, as from July 2024, the Commission continued to be active on the file, and that the matter evolved at national level.

24. The Commission considered the preliminary draft law that Spain shared with it on 24 April 2025 to implement the judgment. However, on 7 October 2025, the draft law, adopted by the Spanish Council of Ministers and sent to the Spanish Congress of Deputies, included some changes compared to the preliminary draft law of April 2025. Those new changes contributed to the delay in ensuring Spain’s compliance with the judgment. Based on the above, the complainant’s argument that no substantive changes were made to the Spanish documentation since 2023 does not appear substantiated.

25. Based on the inquiry, the Ombudsman finds that, as of July 2024, the Commission actively exchanged with Spain on the matter up to 11 December 2025, when the Commission sent another letter of formal notice to Spain. This shows the Commission’s continued efforts to take action aimed at ensuring Spain’s compliance with the judgment.

26. In view of the above, and despite the period of one year without any proof of action by the Commission, the Ombudsman considers that the Commission has overall acted reasonably in following the matter, given the circumstances of this case and in particular the time needed by Spain to complete the legislative process.

27. Although the Commission’s internal guidelines are an important tool for ensuring progress, the Ombudsman considers reasonable the Commission’s explanations that, when a Member State shows willingness to take action, a dialogue may be the most efficient way forward. In this case, the timeline and explanations of the steps taken do not lead to a finding that the Commission manifestly acted outside its discretion in this regard.

28. As a result, although the matter has not yet reached a final conclusion, the Ombudsman considers that no further inquiries are justified at this stage.

29. The Ombudsman trusts that, following the sending of the letter of formal notice to Spain pursuant to Article 260(2) TFEU, the Commission will keep monitoring Spain’s compliance with the judgment actively, diligently and without further delay. Conclusion Based on the inquiry, the Ombudsman closes this case with the following conclusion: Given the steps taken by the Commission so far, including the recent letter of formal notice, and considering the circumstances of the case that explain the timeline of the Commission’s actions, no further inquiries are justified at this stage . The Ombudsman trusts that the Commission will keep monitoring the matter actively, diligently and without further delay. The complainant and the Commission will be informed of this decision . Teresa Anjinho European Ombudsman Strasbourg, 10/03/2026 [1] https://ec.europa.eu/commission/presscorner/detail/en/memo_17_1577 [2] https://ec.europa.eu/commission/presscorner/detail/en/memo_18_349 [3] https://ec.europa.eu/commission/presscorner/detail/en/ip_19_6299 [4] Ley 40/2015 de Régimen Jurídico del Sector Público (Law 40/2015 on the legal system governing the public sector) and Ley 39/2015 del Procedimiento Administrativo Común de las Administraciones Públicas (Law 39/2015 on the common administrative procedure of the public authorities). [5] The complainant had already turned to the Ombudsman in April 2023 and February 2024 about how the Commission was ensuring Spain’s compliance with the judgment. At that time, the Ombudsman found insufficient grounds to inquire into the matter. [6] According to Article 260(2) TFEU, if the Commission considers that the Member State concerned has not taken the necessary measures to comply with the judgment of the Court, it may bring the case before the Court after giving that State the opportunity to submit its observations. [7] See, for instance, of the Court of Justice of the European Union of 25 February 2021, European Commission V Kingdom of Spain , C-658/19, paragraph 19: https://infocuria.curia.europa.eu/tabs/document/C/2019/C-0658-19-00000000RD-01-P-01/ARRET/238164-EN-1-html [8] See, for instance, judgment of the Court of Justice of the European Union, Commission of the European Communities v French Republic , C-334/94 , paragraph 31: https://infocuria.curia.europa.eu/tabs/document/C/1994/C-0334-94-00000000RD-01-P-01/ARRET/100064-EN-1-pdf [9] See, for instance, judgment of the Court of Justice of the European Union, Commission of the European Communities v French Republic , C-334/94 , paragraph 31: https://infocuria.curia.europa.eu/tabs/document/C/1994/C-0334-94-00000000RD-01-P-01/ARRET/100064-EN-1-pdf
summary: The case concerned the time taken by the European Commission to ensure Spain’s compliance with a judgment of the Court of Justice of the European Union concerning Spain’s national rules as regards State liability for breaches of EU law. The complainant was concerned that the Commission was not taking timely and effective actions to ensure Spain’s compliance with the judgment. The Ombudsman found that the Commission had generally followed the matter actively from the adoption of the judgment. Although there was a period of approximately one year showing no documented trace of any action taken by the Commission, the time taken to pursue the matter could be partly attributed to the situation in Spain. In addition, the Ombudsman found reasonable the Commission’s position that, when a Member State shows willingness to take action, a dialogue may be the most efficient way forward. The Ombudsman therefore concluded that, given the steps taken by the Commission so far, including the recent letter of formal notice sent to Spain, and considering the circumstances of the case that explain the timeline of the Commission’s actions, no further inquiries were justified at that point in time and closed the case.

title: Decision concerning complaint 469/2026/MIK against the European Commission
text: Dear Ms X, You submitted a complaint to the European Ombudsman, on behalf of Corporate Europe Observatory, against the European Commission concerning how it prepared the legislative proposal for a regulation simplifying certain requirements and procedures for chemical products (COM(2025)526, Chemicals Omnibus - Omnibus VI). [1] I would like to thank you for bringing this case to my attention. In your complaint, you argue that the Commission failed to comply with EU primary law and its ‘Better Regulation’ rules. In particular, you point out that the Commission failed to carry out a public consultation in relation to the proposal. You also contend that, while preparing it, the Commission relied largely on information provided by the industry concerned. Moreover, you consider that the Commission acted in urgency without proper justification, and that it proposed measures that will undermine the protection of public health and the environment. My Office has recently received several complaints concerning the Commission’s compliance with its ‘Better Regulation’ rules in preparing legislative proposals that it deemed urgent. The issues raised in these complaints are very similar to those you have brought to my attention. Last year, I opened inquiries into three such complaints, [2] and these inquiries cover, among other issues, the Commission’s failure to adequately justify the urgency of its legislative proposals while derogating from the preparation of these proposals’ full impact assessments, and the Commission’s failure to ensure wide and balanced consultation of its proposals. On 25 November 2025, I found [3] that how the Commission had prepared the legislative proposals at issue in the three complaints constituted maladministration and I issued two recommendations and four suggestions for improvement to the Commission, aimed at addressing the shortcomings I identified. Specifically, I recommended that the Commission should ensure a predictable, consistent and non-arbitrary application of its Better Regulation rules, by defining the notion of ‘urgency’ and providing clear explanations for situations that justify a derogation from the requirements set out in the rules. Furthermore, where derogations are granted, the Commission should establish a procedure to ensure that the urgent preparation of legislative proposals still complies with the principles of a transparent, evidence-based and inclusive law-making process. To assist the Commission in this task, I also made four suggestions for improvement, which include clarifying its stakeholder consultation rules for urgent proposals and ensuring that the evidence supporting its proposals is published in good time to enable a public debate before legislation is adopted. I obtained the Commission’s opinion on my recommendations and suggestions on 18 February 2026. [4] My Office is currently analysing this opinion in detail. You can follow the progress of these inquires on my website, where I have published the Commission’s opinion and where my decision will also be published. [5] While I attach great importance to the issues you have raised in your complaint, I also note that they are largely covered by my above recommendations and suggestions. Therefore, I consider that, at this stage, a separate inquiry into your complaint is not warranted. [6] I have therefore closed the case. That said, I will consider the specific arguments you raise in your complaint in the context of analysing the Commission’s opinion on my recommendations. I have also decided to inform the Commission of your complaint, as it suggests that the issues addressed in my recommendation are not limited to the legislative proposals in question in the three cases I inquired into. As you will also be aware, the Commission has launched a call for evidence on its better regulation framework, inviting interested stakeholders to provide feedback on how its better regulation policy could be further improved to remain fit for purpose and future challenges. While the timeline for responding has passed, you can find more information on the Commission’s ‘ Have Your Say ’ consultation website [7] . I hope you will find these explanations useful. Yours sincerely, Teresa Anjinho European Ombudsman Strasbourg, 09/03/2026 [1] https://single-market-economy.ec.europa.eu/publications/simplification-certain-requirements-and-procedures-chemical-products_en [2] Complaint 983/2025/MAS, complaint 2031/2025/VB and complaint 1379/2024/MIK. [3] Recommendation on the European Commission’s compliance with ‘Better Regulation’ rules and other procedural requirements in preparing legislative proposals that it considered to be urgent (983/2025/MAS - the “Omnibus” case, 2031/2024/VB - the “migration” case, and 1379/2024/MIK - the “CAP” case), https://www.ombudsman.europa.eu/en/recommendation/en/215920 . [4] https://www.ombudsman.europa.eu/en/doc/correspondence/en/220471 [5] https://www.ombudsman.europa.eu/en/case/en/68856 [6] Full information on the procedure and rights pertaining to complaints can be found at https://www.ombudsman.europa.eu/en/document/70707 . [7] https://ec.europa.eu/info/law/better-regulation/have-your-say/initiatives/16232-Communication-on-better-regulation_en

title: Decision on how the European Border and Coast Guard Agency (Frontex) carried out a recruitment procedure for standing corps – Intermediate level, AST4 (RCT-2023-00021) (case 1190/2024/KT)
text:
Background to the complaint 1. In October 2023, the complainant participated in a recruitment procedure organised by the European Border and Coast Guard Agency (Frontex) for temporary staff of its standing corps. [1] As part of the recruitment procedure, the complainant had to take an online eliminatory multiple-choice test (‘MCQ test’).

2. In December 2023, Frontex informed the complainant that he had failed the MCQ test. The complainant had scored 15/30 (50%) in the overall test but had failed in section IV of the MCQ (IT Skills), where he had scored 1/6 (16,6%), while the threshold to pass the MCQ was set at 50% in the overall test and 50% in each of its separate sections.

3. The complainant then pointed out to Frontex that at no point in the recruitment procedure had the above requirement (minimum score of 50% in each section of the test) been shared with him. He asked for his score in each section of the test.

4. Frontex informed the complainant that he had passed the threshold in three of the four sections of the test (and had obtained a 50% score in the overall test). Frontex added that the thresholds had been established by the selection committee [2] at the early stages of the recruitment procedure.

5. In January 2024, the complainant made an administrative complaint to Frontex under Article 90(2) of the EU Staff Regulations [3] . He primarily contended [4] that his exclusion from the recruitment procedure was unfair because the recruitment notice did not set out that the requirement to pass the MCQ test entailed a score of 50% in each separate section; nor did other communications from Frontex prior to the MCQ test. The complainant argued that, since he had obtained the minimum overall score required in the test and had also succeeded in the online English language test [5] , Frontex’s decision to exclude him from further participation in the recruitment procedure constituted maladministration.

6. In May 2024, Frontex replied that, according to settled case-law [6] , the threshold for being considered for the next steps of the procedure does not have to be established in advance and set down in the recruitment notice. Such a threshold is not part of the conditions of eligibility for the post, nor part of the presentation of the different stages of the recruitment procedure that were sufficiently described in the recruitment notice.

7. Frontex added that setting the threshold for passing a test falls within the selection committee’s broad discretion. It also makes it possible to deal effectively with recruitment procedures that have attracted a high number of applications, as it helps to limit the number of candidates admitted to the final stage of the recruitment procedure.

8. Frontex confirmed its decision to exclude the complainant from further participation in the recruitment procedure.

9. Dissatisfied with Frontex’s reply, the complainant turned to the Ombudsman in June 2024. The inquiry 10. The Ombudsman opened an inquiry into the complaint to seek clarifications about the MCQ test and, in particular, the requirement of a minimum score of 50% in each of its separate sections.

11. In the course of the inquiry, the Ombudsman received Frontex’s reply to certain questions. The Ombudsman’s inquiry team also inspected Frontex's file on this case. Following Frontex’s reply to the first set of questions and the inspection of the first set of documents, the Ombudsman’s inquiry team asked Frontex additional questions and inspected additional documents. [7] Arguments presented to the Ombudsman 12. The complainant reiterated the arguments he had put forward in his administrative complaint to Frontex. [8] He contended that Frontex arbitrarily introduced an additional important requirement (a threshold for each of the separate sections of the MCQ test) which was never communicated to candidates. The complainant considered himself unfairly excluded from the recruitment procedure, as he had reached the overall threshold for the MCQ test. He claimed that he should be admitted to the next step of the recruitment procedure.

13. The complainant added that there was no need to introduce such a discriminatory criterion to reduce the number of candidates: the recruitment procedure comprised several subsequent stages that would anyway end up reducing significantly the number of candidates. The complainant wanted to know how the decision to introduce this requirement was taken.

14. Frontex stated that the selection committee had discussed the thresholds (50% in the overall MCQ test and 50% in each of its separate sections) in a meeting on 28 September 2023. The respective decision was recorded in the minutes of that meeting, that were validated with no objections the day after.

15. Frontex added that, according to EU case-law, [9] where a recruitment notice describes in detail both the profile of the vacant post, the essential and the advantageous criteria for filling that post, as well as the various steps of the recruitment procedure, but does not define the method for assessing the tests, this means that, within the legal framework established by the recruitment notice, the appointing authority intended to allow the selection committee some discretion. In exercising this discretion, the selection committee established, in an objective manner, prior to the assessment of the applications received, the threshold for each section of the MCQ test. The Ombudsman's assessment 16. The Ombudsman notes that the recruitment notice did not provide any information on the structure and marking of the MCQ test, such as what would be required to “pass” the test. The same goes for the instructions given to candidates before the test, as well as for the individual invitation to the test, both of which contained information only about the technical aspects of the test.

17. According to EU case-law, a selection committee has a wide discretion in deciding the rules and conditions under which a recruitment procedure is organised. [10] Such conditions should, in general, be clearly set out in the recruitment notice.

18. In particular, according to EU case-law, the function of the recruitment notice is, first, to give the most accurate information possible on the nature of the conditions of eligibility for the post to be filled, and second, to establish the legal framework within which the comparative assessment of the merits of the candidates will be carried out. The latter implies that sufficiently precise requirements must be set out to enable that comparison and to justify the choices made. [11] The threshold (“pass mark”) for being considered for the next steps of a recruitment procedure does not have to be established in advance and set down in the recruitment notice. On the contrary, this falls within the broad discretion of the selection committee, which makes it possible, among other things, in case of a large number of applications, to limit the number of candidates admitted to the next stage of the procedure. [12] 19. The Ombudsman thus considers that, in this case, the recruitment notice did not have to set out the threshold for being successful in the MCQ test. Rather, the selection committee had the discretion to do so at a later stage.

20. However, the Ombudsman notes that the discretion of a selection committee in such cases is not unlimited. It is essential that the selection committee establishes any thresholds before it has any knowledge of the candidates’ individual performance . This is to ensure a fair and objective recruitment procedure and to dispel any concern that the procedure was tailored to favour or disadvantage certain candidates.

21. As mentioned above [13] , Frontex stated that the selection committee had set the thresholds for the MCQ test on 29 September 2023. This was before the MCQ test took place on 10 October 2023.

22. The inspection of documents confirmed that the selection committee had indeed decided on the requirement of a minimum score of 50% in each of the separate sections of the MCQ test before the test took place. This means that, at the time when the selection committee decided on the required pass mark, it could not know which of the candidates would meet that requirement. As such, the Ombudsman finds no maladministration in how Frontex set the threshold for the MCQ test.

23. That said, the Ombudsman notes that, while the selection committee decided on the thresholds after the recruitment notice was published , it did so well ahead of the MCQ test . In particular, as mentioned above, the selection committee established these thresholds on 29 September 2023 and the test took place on 10 October 2023. This means that Frontex had enough time to inform candidates in advance about the requirements to pass the MCQ test.

24. Although not a legal requirement, informing candidates beforehand that a particular pass mark would be required for each section, would enhance transparency, allowing candidates to know beforehand what level of performance was required in each area and to understand how success or failure in the MCQ test was determined. It would also allow them to better prepare their strategy during the test (for example, how to allocate time and effort across the various sections of the MCQ test). The Ombudsman will thus make a suggestion for improvement to Frontex in that regard.

25. The Ombudsman further notes that the inspection of Frontex’s file showed that how Frontex kept records of the work of the selection committee in this recruitment procedure was problematic. In particular, the minutes of the meetings of the selection committee were kept in the form of PowerPoint deck slides, which often lacked the appropriate degree of clarity, accuracy and completeness.

26. The Ombudsman recalls that the core purpose of record-keeping in recruitment procedures is to provide a clear record of the process followed and the decisions taken by the selection committee. This serves transparency and accountability purposes, as it allows the selection committee to justify its work and defend the outcome of the recruitment procedure if questioned or challenged later.

27. The Ombudsman notes that Frontex’s record-keeping in this case was not such as to allow an external review body - such as the Ombudsman - to easily obtain a clear picture of the work by the selection committee. This made the inspection of the file laborious and prolonged the duration of the Ombudsman’s inquiry. In addition, although Frontex was requested, at the opening of the inquiry, to provide for inspection all documents relating to the work of the selection committee as regards the MCQ test, the relevant documents were provided in a scattered manner and only following further requests and delays.

28. This is not the first time that the issue of how Frontex keeps records of the internal work of selection committees in its recruitment procedures arises. In the context of a previous inquiry against Frontex concerning two recruitment procedures, the Ombudsman had identified shortcomings in record-keeping in other areas of the selection committee’s work [14] and had made a relevant suggestion, underlining that “ keeping adequate records is important for transparency and also facilitates the work of any external review body that might be subsequently involved (the Ombudsman or the EU Courts)” . [15] 29. While acknowledging the improvements made by Frontex in how it manages its recruitment procedures over the last years, the Ombudsman considers that this case demonstrates that, as regards record-keeping, there is still room for improvement. In future, Frontex should ensure that selection committees in its staff recruitment procedures record their internal work in a sufficiently clear and accessible manner, to allow any external review body to draw conclusions on key aspects of the selection process easily and promptly. The Ombudsman will thus make to Frontex a relevant suggestion for improvement . Conclusion Based on the inquiry, the Ombudsman closes this case with the following conclusion: There was no maladministration in how Frontex set the thresholds for the eliminatory online multiple-choice test. Suggestions for improvement 1. Where possible, Frontex should consider informing candidates in its recruitment procedures of the threshold(s) for any eliminatory test prior to testing, to enhance transparency and allow candidates to better prepare for the test.

2. Frontex should ensure that selection committees in its staff recruitment procedures record their internal work in a sufficiently clear and accessible manner, to allow any external review body to draw conclusions on key aspects of the selection process easily and promptly. Frontex should inform the Ombudsman within three months of this decision of the measures it has put in place in response to these suggestions. The complainant and Frontex will be informed of this decision . Teresa Anjinho European Ombudsman Strasbourg, 24/02/2026 [1] Recruitment notice RCT-2023-00021, available at: https://microsite.frontex.europa.eu/en/recruitments/RCT-2023-00021 [2] Each selection procedure has a selection board or selection committee , which is responsible for selecting candidates at each stage, based on pre-determined criteria, and for drawing up the final list of successful candidates. [3] Regulation No 31 (EEC), 11 (EAEC), laying down the Staff Regulations of Officials and the Conditions of Employment of Other Servants of the European Economic Community and the European Atomic Energy Community, available at: https://eur-lex.europa.eu/legal-content/EN/TXT/?uri=CELEX%3A01962R0031-20260101 [4] The complainant raised also other issues, which, however, do not form part of the Ombudsman’s inquiry in this case. [5] This English language test, which the complainant took the day after the MCQ test, was also part of the recruitment procedure. [6] Frontex referred to the judgement of the General Court of 8 May 2019, T-99/18, Stamatopoulos v ENISA. [7] The concern raised by the complaint in this case was also raised with the Ombudsman by another candidate in the same recruitment procedure in the context of another case (232/2024/KT). In the course of the inquiry in that case, the Ombudsman had requested Frontex to provide a detailed written reply to certain questions, as well as some internal documents for inspection. While the Ombudsman ultimately closed the inquiry into that case because the complainant subsequently withdrew his complaint, Frontex was informed that any information and documents it had provided in the context of case 232/2024/KT would be taken into consideration and assessed in the context of this case. Thus, the Ombudsman’s inquiry into this case takes into consideration also the documents received in the context of the closed case 232/2024/KT and, in particular, Frontex’s reply to the Ombudsman in the context of that case. [8] See paragraph 5 above. [9] Frontex referred to the judgement of the Civil Service Tribunal of 11 December 2012, Trentea v FRA , F-112/10, and to the judgement of the General Court of 1 June 2022, OG v EDA, T-632/20. [10] Judgment of the Civil Service Tribunal of 24 April 2013, CB v Commission , F-73/11, paragraph 81, available at https://infocuria.curia.europa.eu/tabs/document/F/2011/F-0073-11-00000000RF-01-P-01/ARRET/136722-EN-1-html [11] Judgment of the General Court of 8 May 2019, T-99/18, Stamatopoulos v ENISA , paragraph 48, available at https://infocuria.curia.europa.eu/tabs/document/T/2018/T-0099-18-00000000RF-01-P-01/ARRET_NP/213849-EN-1-html [12] Judgement in case T-99/18, cited above, paragraph 49. [13] See paragraph 14. [14] See the Ombudsman’s closing decision in case 174/2021/KT, available at: https://www.ombudsman.europa.eu/en/decision/en/163564 [15] Paragraph 26 of the Ombudsman’s decision cited above.
summary: The case concerned a recruitment procedure organised by the European Border and Coast Guard Agency (Frontex) for temporary staff of its standing corps. The complainant, a candidate in this procedure, had obtained the minimum overall score required for an eliminatory multiple-choice test (‘MCQ test’). However, Frontex excluded him from further participation in the recruitment procedure because he had not obtained the minimum score in each of the separate sections of the MCQ test. The complainant contended that his exclusion from the recruitment procedure was unfair because this requirement, which he considered arbitrary, had not been communicated to candidates before the MCQ test. The Ombudsman considered that Frontex had discretion to establish the thresholds for the MCQ test at a later stage of the recruitment procedure, provided that it did so before knowing candidates’ individual performances. As Frontex had set the thresholds before the test took place, the Ombudsman found that there was no maladministration. Nevertheless, the Ombudsman suggested that, where possible, Frontex consider informing candidates in its recruitment procedures of the thresholds for any eliminatory test prior to testing, to enhance transparency and allow candidates to better prepare for the test. The Ombudsman further identified deficiencies in how Frontex kept records of the internal work done by the selection committee. The Ombudsman thus suggested that, for transparency and accountability reasons, Frontex improve the standards of clarity and accessibility to such records.

title: Decision on how the European Union Agency for Asylum  replied to a request for feedback from a candidate in a selection procedure for recruiting an operations officer (EUAA/2023/CA/002) (case 476/2025/RVK)
text:
Background to the complaint 1. The complainant took part in a selection procedure, which was organised by the European Union Agency for Asylum (EUAA) to recruit an operations officer (Contract Agent at Grade FG IV). [1] 2. On 15 March 2024, the complainant participated in the (online) written test phase, consisting of multiple-choice questions (‘written test’). On 25 March 2024, the EUAA informed the complainant that she was not successful in the written test.

3. The complainant then made a request for feedback. The complainant asked for her score and for the correct answers to the written test, as she considered that obtaining access to this information was in line with the EUAA’s transparency obligations and would allow her to learn from her mistakes.

4. The EUAA provided the complainant with her individual score, the maximum score possible and the minimum score required to pass the threshold for the written test. The EUAA refused to provide the correct answers to the written test, arguing that they are covered by the principle of secrecy [2] . The EUAA thereby considered that it had complied with its duty to state reasons.

5. Dissatisfied with the EUAA’s refusal to provide the correct answers to the written test, the complainant turned to the Ombudsman in February 2025. The inquiry 6. The Ombudsman opened an inquiry into how the EUAA replied to the complainant’s request for feedback. Based on case-law of the Court of Justice of the EU [3] , the Ombudsman asked the EUAA to consider providing the complainant with a list that shows, in the form of letters, the answers given by the complainant, the correct answers, as well as the time devoted by the complainant for each question.

7. In the course of the inquiry, the Ombudsman inquiry team received the EUAA’s reply and the complainant’s comments on that reply. Arguments presented to the Ombudsman 8. The complainant argued, in summary, that obtaining access to the correct answers would allow her to understand her mistakes and improve for future selection procedures. The complainant considered that the written test has definitive, verifiable answers, leaving no room for subjective interpretation or confidentiality concerns. Withholding such information raises serious concerns about transparency and fairness in the selection procedure.

9. The EUAA explained that, for contractual reasons it had not been able to use its usual online test environment for the written test in question. It had therefore sent all candidates the written test in the form a Word document. This document contained the questions and the possible multiple-choice answers.

10. The EUAA acknowledged the case-law referred to by the Ombudsman, but argued that due to the specific test modality used in this case and the information already provided to the complainant, in particular the questions of the written test, it has no obligation to provide the complainant with the correct answers to this test. In the EUAA’s view, disclosure of the correct answers would seriously undermine the secrecy of the selection committee’s proceedings [4] , the principle of equal treatment of candidates and the integrity of future selection procedures in which the questions could be reused. Given that the complainant could well retain the copy of the written test, access to the correct answers could place her, or any other potential recipient, in a more advantageous position than other candidates in future selection procedures.

11. The EUAA furthermore explained that the test modality used did not allow it to determine the time devoted by the complainant to each question.

12. The EUAA thus concluded that it was unable to follow the Ombudsman’s proposal in the specific situation. The EUAA agreed, however, to provide candidates with the information proposed by the Ombudsman, when it uses its standard test modality (that is, when not sending the candidates the test in a Word document), “ insofar technically feasible ”.

13. However, the EUAA proposed to inform the complainant about which questions she had answered incorrectly (without disclosing the correct answers).

14. The EUAA considered that it had complied with its duty to state reasons, while at the same time respecting the principle of secrecy surrounding the selection committee’s proceedings, as set out by the Staff Regulations. The Ombudsman's assessment 15. The Ombudsman has consistently taken the view that, in line with the principles of good administration and based on established case-law, EU bodies should provide candidates, upon request, with a grid of questions asked during the tests, which shows, in the form of letters, the answers given by the candidate, the correct answers as well as the time devoted by the candidate for each question.

16. The Ombudsman notes that, in this selection procedure, the EUAA could not use its standard online test modality. As a result, it had to provide the candidates with the questions to the written test and the corresponding possible multiple-choice answers, in the form of a Word document. In that particular context, the Ombudsman finds reasonable the EUAA’s explanation that it cannot provide information about the time devoted to each question. Although unfortunate, in these circumstances, the Ombudsman also finds reasonable the EUAA’s position that it cannot provide, in the form of letters, the correct answers as well as the answers given by the complainant. The Ombudsman accepts the EUAA’s view that, in the given situation, where the complainant could retain a Word version of the test including the questions, having also access to the correct answers could put the complainant, or any other potential recipient of the information, in a more advantageous position than other candidates in future selection procedures, where the questions may be reused.

17. The Ombudsman welcomes the EUAA’s willingness to provide the information in question when using its standard online modality for written tests.

18. The Ombudsman furthermore welcomes the EUAA’s proposal to share with the complainant information about which questions she had answered incorrectly. The Ombudsman considers that having access to this information would allow the complainant to have a substantive amount of information about her performance, also taking into consideration that the complainant could retain a copy of the written test.

19. In light of the above, the Ombudsman considers there are no further inquiries justified into how the EUAA replied to the complainant’s request for feedback. Conclusion Based on the inquiry, the Ombudsman closes this case with the following conclusion [5] : In light of the EUAA’s explanation—that, due to the specific test format and the need to safeguard future selection procedures, it could not disclose to the complainant the correct answers, while offering to indicate the questions answered incorrectly—the Ombudsman considers that no further inquiries are justified into how the EUAA replied to the complainant’s request for feedback . The complainant and the EUAA will be informed of this decision . Teresa Anjinho European Ombudsman Strasbourg, 24/02/2026 [1] This concerns vacancy notice EUAA/2023/CA/002. More information available at https://eu-careers.europa.eu/en/job-opportunities/operations-officer/euaa-2023-ca-002 . [2] See Article 6 of Annex III of the Staff Regulations: “The proceedings of the Selection Board shall be secret.” Available at https://eur-lex.europa.eu/legal-content/EN/TXT/PDF/?uri=CELEX:01962R0031-20240101 [3] See joint cases T‑515/14 P and T‑516/14 Alexandrou v. European Commission para 84. https://curia.europa.eu/juris/document/document.jsf?text=&docid=171421&pageIndex=0&doclang=FR&mode=lst&d ir=&occ=first&part=1&cid=10312923 and case T-596/18 ZL v. European Union Intellectual Property Office (EUIPO) para 50 https://curia.europa.eu/juris/document/document.jsf?text=&docid=231529&pageIndex=0&doclang=en&mode=req&dir=&occ=first&part=1 : “ [...] in order for candidates to be properly informed, it is sufficient, as a general rule, to communicate to them after the test a grid stating, with regard to the number corresponding to each question, the answer given (in the form of a letter), the correct answer (also in the form of a letter) and the time which the candidate devoted to answering the question. ” [4] See Article 6 of Annex III of the Staff Regulations: “The proceedings of the Selection Board shall be secret.” Available at https://eur-lex.europa.eu/legal-content/EN/TXT/PDF/?uri=CELEX:01962R0031-20240101 [5] This complaint has been dealt with under delegated case handling, in accordance with the Decision of the European Ombudsman adopting Implementing Provisions
summary: The case concerned how the European Union Agency for Asylum (EUAA) replied to the complainant’s request for feedback in a selection procedure for recruiting an operations officer. The complainant has asked the EUAA for the correct answers to the multiple-choice test, which the EUAA refused to provide. In the context of the inquiry, the Ombudsman asked the EUAA to consider providing the complainant with a list that shows, in the form of letters, the answers given by the complainant, the correct answers, as well as the time devoted by the complainant for each question. The EUAA replied that it was unable to do so given the particular nature of the specific test, as the candidates had been provided with a Word document with the questions and the multiple-choice answers. However, the EUAA was prepared to inform the complainant about which questions she had not answered correctly. The Ombudsman found the EUAA’s explanations reasonable in the given context and therefore closed the case with the conclusion that no further inquiries are justified.

title: How the Council of the EU dealt with a request for public access to documents concerning the language regime in the EU
text: Dear Mr X, You submitted a complaint to the European Ombudsman about how the Council of the EU dealt with a request for public access to documents concerning the language regime in the EU. The Ombudsman opened an inquiry into the Council’s refusal to give public access and requested to inspect the documents at issue. The Council has now informed the Ombudsman that, in the context of this inquiry, it has carried out a new assessment of the three documents at issue in your confirmatory application. As a result, the Council has decided to grant full access to the three documents. You remain dissatisfied with the Council’s confirmatory decision to refuse access to the documents. Please note that the Ombudsman’s inquiry concerned the refusal to grant public access to the documents. Given that the Council has reassessed its position and has now granted full access to the three documents, the case is closed with the conclusion that it has been settled . Yours sincerely, Rosita Hickey Director of Inquiries Strasbourg, 20/02/2026

title: Decision on how the European Commission dealt with a request for public access to documents concerning the implementation of a court judgment related to European harmonised standards (case 437/2025/MIK)
text:
Background 1. ‘Harmonised standards’ are technical specifications defining requirements for products, production processes, services or test-methods. European harmonised standards are developed by one of the European Standardisation Organisations, that is, the European Committee for Standardization (CEN), the European Committee for Electrotechnical Standardization (CENELEC), and the European Telecommunications Standards Institute (ETSI). European standards are aligned as much as possible with international standards adopted by the recognised International Standardisation Organisations. [1] 2. Although harmonised standards are not legally binding as such, products which comply with them may benefit from a ‘presumption of conformity’ with the underlying EU legislation concerning the specific products. The Court of Justice acknowledged that, in practice, compliance with harmonised standards may be the only viable way for economic operators to ensure that their products comply with EU legislation, [2] as coming up with a different way of complying with EU legislation would be too costly. This is why “ harmonised standards form part of EU law ”. [3] 3. Given this legal status of harmonised standards, the Court of Justice considered, in its judgment in case Public.Resource.Org Inc. v Commission of March 2024, that there is an overriding public interest in disclosure of harmonised standards [4] under the EU legislation on public access to documents (Regulation 1049/2001). [5] This means that the Commission must disclose harmonised standards even if certain exceptions to public access might be applicable, including the exception concerning the protection of the commercial interests [6] of the European Standardisation Organisations. According to the Court of Justice, the principle of the rule of law [7] requires free access to all EU law - including harmonised standards - for all natural or legal persons in the EU. [8] 4. Public access to harmonised standards is currently ensured through web portals set up by CEN and CENELEC, [9] as well as ETSI. [10] The Commission provides public access to some standards upon request. [11] 5. Against this background, the complainant requested public access to all internal assessments of the Commission’s implementation of the judgment Public.Resource.Org Inc. v Commission.

6. In its initial reply, the Commission identified two documents as falling within the scope of the complainant's request: (1) a note by its Directorate-General for Internal Market, Industry, Entrepreneurship and SMEs (DG GROW) to the Secretariat-General and legal service of the Commission, of 25 March 2024, and (2) a note of the legal service of the Commission to DG GROW, of 15 April 2024. The Commission refused to disclose these documents in their entirety, invoking exceptions to public access under Regulation 1049/2001 concerning the protection of court proceedings and legal advice, [12] and an ongoing decision-making process. [13] 7. The complainant asked the Commission to reconsider its position by submitting a ‘confirmatory application’.

8. In its confirmatory decision, the Commission identified an additional document as falling within the scope of the request, namely an annex to the first document mentioned above, a proposal from CEN and CENELEC. The Commission maintained its refusal to grant access to all three documents, relying on exceptions related to the protection of privacy and integrity of individuals, [14] court proceedings and legal advice, and an ongoing decision-making process.

9. In particular, the Commission considered that the requested documents were closely linked to ongoing court proceedings [15] concerning how the Commission provides public access to harmonised standards and their publication.

10. Dissatisfied with the Commission’s decision, the complainant turned to the Ombudsman. The inquiry 11. The Ombudsman opened an inquiry into the Commission’s refusal to grant public access to the requested documents. As the complainant did not question the redaction of personal data in the requested documents, the Ombudsman’s inquiry did not cover the Commission’s application of this exception to public access.

12. During the inquiry, the Ombudsman inquiry team inspected the non-redacted versions of the documents at issue and held a meeting with the Commission’s representatives. The Ombudsman also received the complainant’s comments on the meeting report. Arguments presented 13. The complainant emphasised the public interest in having access to harmonised standards. In this context, he considered that obtaining public access to the documents he requested in this case contributes to this overarching goal, given that the documents are likely to reveal how the disclosure of harmonised standards operates in practice. Furthermore, the complainant considered that, following the judgment in case Public.Resource.Org Inc. v Commission , existing EU rules on European standardisation [16] need to be amended to provide for the publication of harmonised standards, in all EU official languages. He argued that the requested documents are part of the Commission’s preparatory work to amend these rules and, therefore, there exists an overriding public interest in disclosure. [17] 14. During the meeting with the Ombudsman inquiry team, the Commission representatives explained that the Commission needed to find a solution to implement the judgment in Public.Resource.Org Inc. v Commission . The documents sought by the complainant concern the Commission’s internal exchanges on the set-up of this solution. Since the judgment, the Commission has received many further requests for public access to harmonised standards, and some have led to legal challenges before the EU Courts. Some parties before the EU Courts consider the Commission’s implementation of the judgment in Public.Resource.Org Inc. v Commission to be too narrow , whereas others consider it too broad. In particular, international standard-setting organisations have challenged the Commission’s implementation because they consider that it infringes their copyright and undermines their commercial interests. [18] The Commission added that the outcome of the ongoing court cases will not only impact the solution now in place in relation to the transparency of harmonised standards, but also pending legislative and policy initiatives, such as a modification of the legal framework regarding standards by the Commission’s proposals COM(2025)503 [19] and COM(2025)504 [20] , as well as the ongoing revision of Regulation (EU) 1025/2012 on European standardisation. [21] 15. In view of this, the Commission invoked in its confirmatory decision several exceptions to public access. Specifically, as regards the protection of court proceedings and legal advice, the Commission argued that disclosing documents containing internal legal advice could undermine the Commission’s ability to receive objective and frank legal advice in the future, as well as the capacity of its legal service to represent the Commission in court proceedings and defend the legality of the Commission’s acts. In this context, the Commission emphasised that, although the requested documents were not drafted for the purpose of the currently ongoing court proceedings, they are closely linked to them and contain sensitive legal opinions. Consequently, their disclosure could affect the equality of arms between the parties before the court, giving one party access to the other party’s internal legal advice.

16. As regards the protection of an ongoing decision-making process, the Commission argued that disclosing the documents could undermine its deliberations and affect its capacity to make informed decisions, given that the implementation of the judgment at issue is still subject to internal discussions and negotiations with third parties.

17. Finally, the Commission could not identify an overriding public interest in disclosure of the requested documents. It said that the requested documents contain ‘ preliminary reflections exchanged between the Commission services in the process of developing an approach to implementing the Court ruling in Case C-588/21P, and thus do not concern legal questions arising when legislative initiatives were being prepared or debated, nor would their disclosure increase the transparency and openness of any legislative process .’ The Ombudsman's assessment 18. The inspection of the documents at issue in this inquiry showed that they contain the Commission’s initial assessment of the judgment in Public.Resource.Org Inc. v Commission. Specifically, one document contains DG GROW’s proposal to the legal service on how to implement the judgment (with the position of CEN and CENELEC annexed), while the second document is the legal service’s reply to that proposal.

19. The Commission refused access to these documents in their entirety, relying mostly on the need to protect ongoing court proceedings, legal advice and its decision-making process. Protection of court proceedings 20. The court proceedings exception can be applied to documents that were not drawn up in the context of specific court proceedings, but which present a relevant link with such ongoing proceedings. Moreover, to be able to rely on the exception, the documents’ disclosure must seriously compromise the integrity of the court proceedings and the principle of equality of arms. The Court of Justice has stated that this could occur “ if parties were to benefit from privileged access to internal information belonging to the other party and closely connected to the legal aspects of pending [...] proceedings ”. [22] 21. In this regard, the Commission argued that disclosing the documents would impede the Commission’s ability to defend itself in ongoing court proceedings by revealing its internal assessment of a previous judgment that is closely linked.

22. The Ombudsman inquiry team inspected the documents at issue, as well as the Commission’s decisions which are currently challenged before the General Court. Moreover, the Ombudsman considered information about a related preliminary reference pending before the Court of Justice. [23] 23. Based on a comparison of the contents of the requested documents and the subject matter of the ongoing court proceedings which the Commission is seeking to protect, it is not clear to the Ombudsman how the equality of arms and the integrity of the court proceedings would be impeded by a partial disclosure of the requested documents. The inspection showed that the documents largely contain general information to which the parties to the court proceedings and the public, including the complainant, seem already to have access. Specifically, the Commission describes on its website [24] how it is currently granting public access to some harmonised standards following the judgment. The remaining information in the requested documents, which is not publicly available, does not appear to be sensitive from the perspective of the ongoing court proceedings, except for specific passages that could be redacted.

24. Overall, the Ombudsman is not convinced that partial disclosure of the requested documents would undermine the integrity of the ongoing court proceedings and the principle of equality of arms. Protection of legal advice 25. In accordance with EU case-law, the legal advice exception can be applied to refuse public access to certain legal opinions if their disclosure undermines the institution’s ability to give and receive frank, objective and comprehensive legal advice. This risk must be reasonably foreseeable and not purely hypothetical. [25] 26. The Commission relied on this exception to refuse public access to the reply of its legal service to DG GROW. The inspection showed that certain parts of the requested document are either general in nature or have already been implemented by the Commission as part of the abovementioned solution to provide public access to harmonised standards. [26] Specific passages that the Commission could reasonably consider sensitive could be redacted. As with the exception regarding the protection of court proceedings, the Ombudsman is not convinced in this context that granting partial access to the document would undermine the Commission’s ability to give and receive frank, objective and comprehensive legal advice in the future. Protection of an ongoing decision-making process 27. The Commission furthermore invoked the protection of an ongoing decision-making process to refuse access to the three documents. Based on the inquiry, it appears that the Commission is mainly concerned about external pressure that stakeholders might exercise on the Commission if they knew the details of its internal assessment of the judgment.

28. While targeted external pressure may constitute a legitimate ground for refusing access to a document, as the EU Courts have emphasised, the reality of such external pressure must be established with certainty, and it must be demonstrated that there was a reasonably foreseeable risk of the process being substantially affected owing to that external pressure. [27] In this regard, the Ombudsman has repeatedly stated that a reference to a generic risk of ‘external pressure’ cannot in itself justify non-disclosure. [28] While the Commission provided some additional confidential explanations in this regard, the Ombudsman remains unconvinced that partial disclosure would result in such external pressure to the extent that it could undermine the Commission’s ability to implement the court’s judgment concerning the disclosure of European harmonised standards.

29. In view of the above, the Ombudsman is not convinced that the Commission was justified in refusing access to the requested documents in their entirety, based on the above exceptions. There was thus no need for the Ombudsman to assess whether there is an overriding public interest in disclosure of the documents.

30. That said, and as noted above, information about how the Commission is implementing the judgment in Public.Resource.Org Inc. v Commission is already in the public domain. [29] In the light of the information already in the public domain and the inspection of the relevant documents, the Ombudsman does not see a useful purpose in continuing her inquiry. The Ombudsman would suggest, however, that the Commission reconsider its position based on her observations put forward in this decision, with a view to still granting partial access to the requested documents. Conclusion Based on the inquiry, the Ombudsman closes this case with the following conclusion: The Ombudsman is not convinced by the Commission’s position that partial access to the requested documents was prevented by the need to protect ongoing court proceedings, legal advice and a decision-making process. However, given that information about how the Commission has implemented the judgment in Public.Resource.Org Inc. v Commission is already in the public domain, the Ombudsman considers that no further inquiries are justified in this case. The complainant and the Commission will be informed of this decision . Suggestion for improvement The Commission should reconsider its position on the access request and grant at least partial access to the requested documents. summary: The case concerned how the European Commission dealt with a request for public access to documents concerning the implementation of a judgment related to European harmonised standards. In its judgment, the Court of Justice considered these standards, which are developed by European Standardisation Organisations upon the request of the Commission, to form part of EU law, given their significant legal effects. As a result, the Court held that the Commission must make practical arrangements for disclosing these standards to the public. The complainant sought access to the Commission’s internal assessment of the Court’s judgment. The Commission refused access to the documents it identified as falling within the scope of the request, invoking the need to protect legal advice and ongoing court proceedings, as well as its decision-making process. In particular, the Commission argued that its internal assessment is closely linked to several cases still pending before the EU Courts. Based on an inspection of the documents, the Ombudsman found that they largely contain general information to which the public and the parties to the ongoing court proceedings invoked by the Commission may already have access. While the Ombudsman was not convinced by the Commission’s position that partial access to the requested documents could not have been granted, she found that no further inquiries were justified in this case, given that information regarding how the Commission has implemented the judgment is already in the public domain. The Ombudsman suggested, however, that the Commission reconsider its position on the access request in light of her observations, with a view to granting partial access.

title: Decision in the above case on how the European Commission dealt with a request for public access to documents concerning investigations into violations of fundamental rights of third country nationals in Greece
text: Dear Ms X, You submitted to the European Ombudsman a complaint concerning how the European Commission dealt with a request for public access to documents [1] made in July 2025 and registered under the reference EASE 2025/3404. The Commission did not reply to your initial access request within the applicable time limits. Therefore, on 20 August 2025, you submitted a confirmatory application challenging the Commission’s implicit refusal to grant access. The Commission extended the time limit to reply to your confirmatory application until 1 October 2025. In the absence of an explicit reply to your access request, you turned to the Ombudsman. On 9 January 2026, the Ombudsman opened an inquiry into the Commission’s implicit refusal to give public access to the documents at issue. [2] As a first inquiry step, the Ombudsman requested that the Commission reply to the confirmatory application as soon as possible. In January 2026, the Commission informed the Ombudsman’s Office that it had adopted an explicit decision. The Commission decided to grant full public access to some of the documents and redacted only personal data [3] in others. For one document, the Commission granted partial access, invoking the need to protect the public interest as regards public security [4] . At the same time, the Secretariat-General of the Commission indicated to you that the decision now adopted constituted an initial reply, and that it will “ close ” the confirmatory application related to the lack of initial reply. The letter specified that you remained entitled to submit a new confirmatory application in accordance with Article 7(2) of the EU legislation on public access to documents (Regulation 1049/2001). In your comments on the Commission’s reply, you said that you were not able to locate the Commission’s reply. In addition, you considered that further documents should have been identified as falling within the scope of the access request. For that reason, you decided to make a new confirmatory application on 29 January 2026. Overall, you considered that the handling of your access request amounts to maladministration given that you have still not received the requested documents. Upon the Ombudsman Office’s request, the Commission resent its reply to you. Given that you have made a new confirmatory application, the Ombudsman considers that the Commission should be given the opportunity to review its position within the applicable time limits. Therefore, no further inquiries into this complaint are justified at this stage. The Ombudsman has therefore closed the case. If the Commission does not reply to your second confirmatory application within the time limits set out in Regulation 1049/2001, or if you are dissatisfied with the Commission’s reply, you can make another complaint to the Ombudsman. That said, the Ombudsman has reminded [5] the Commission that requesters should be informed that they can immediately turn to the Ombudsman where the Commission adopts a decision following a confirmatory application that was made based on an implicit refusal at initial stage. As mentioned when opening the inquiry into your complaint, the Ombudsman did not inquire into the reasons for the Commission’s delay in replying to you. However, the Ombudsman brought your concerns in this regard to the Commission’s attention. She is aware that the issue of the Commission’s delays in dealing with public access requests persists and she is closely monitoring the matter based on complaints submitted to her. Yours sincerely, Rosita Hickey Director of Inquiries Strasbourg, 18/02/2026 [1] Under Regulation 1049/2001 regarding public access to European Parliament, Council and Commission documents: http://data.europa.eu/eli/reg/2001/1049/oj . [2] See Article 8(3) of Regulation 1049/2001 according to which, in the absence of an explicit reply within the timeline provided, this gives rise to an implicit negative reply to the request for review. [3] In accordance with Article 4(1)(b) of Regulation 1049/2001. [4] In accordance with Article 4(1)(a), first indent of Regulation 1049/2001. [5] See recommendation in case OI/2/2022/OAM, paragraphs 51 to 53, available at: https://www.ombudsman.europa.eu/en/recommendation/en/167661 .

title: Decision on how the European Commission dealt with a request for public access to documents concerning the use of EU funding for security-related activities in Africa
text: Dear Dr X, You submitted a complaint to the European Ombudsman about how the European Commission dealt with a request for public access to documents [1] related to possible EU funding [2] for security-related activities in Africa (EASE 2024/6909). The Ombudsman opened an inquiry into the Commission’s implicit refusal to give public access under Regulation 1049/2001. As a first inquiry step, the Ombudsman asked the Commission to reply to your confirmatory application. The Commission adopted its confirmatory decision within the given time limit. The Ombudsman notes that at confirmatory stage, the Commission granted full access to two documents and maintained its position that further disclosure of the remaining documents would undermine the protection of (i) the public interest as regards international relations [3] and (ii) legal advice. [4] The Ombudsman asked you to provide comments on the extent of public access thus granted. You did not provide any comments. Given that this inquiry concerned the Commission’s implicit refusal to give public access and in the absence of comments as regards the access granted, the case is now closed with the conclusion that it has been settled . Yours sincerely, Rosita Hickey Director of Inquiries Strasbourg, 17/02/2026 [1] Under Regulation 1049/2001 regarding public access to European Parliament, Council and Commission documents: http://data.europa.eu/eli/reg/2001/1049/oj .
